revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23985667504|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
[[fr:Hinumanai-jinja]][[ja:比沼麻奈為神社]] {{Infobox religious building | native_name = 比沼麻奈為神社 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Hinumanai-jinja, haiden.jpg |map_type=Japan| coordinates = {{Coord|35.592583|N|135.028972|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Toyouke-hime|en|Toyouke-hime|qid=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke-hime|1=Toyouke-hime}} | festival = September 15th | prefecture= {{ill|Kyotango|qid=Q513897|1=Kyōtango}} }} {{Nihongo|{{ill|Hinumanai Shrine|ja|比沼麻奈為神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinumanai Shrine|qq=}}|比沼麻奈為神社}} is a shrine to {{ill|Toyouke-hime|en|Toyouke-hime|qid=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke-hime|1=Toyouke-hime}} in {{ill|Kyōtango|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōtango|qq=}}.{{Cite web|title=Dragons, Demons and Deities: Folklore of the Kyoto by the Sea Area {{!|qq=draft}} Inspiration |url=https://www.kyototourism.org/en/column/17773/ |access-date=2023-10-03 |website=Another Kyoto Official Travel Guide |language=en-US}}{{Cite web|last=Okunomichi|date=2017-08-06|title=Tanba no Kuni: Hinumanai Jinja|url=https://yamanomiya.wordpress.com/2017/08/06/875/|access-date=2024-03-29|website=Yama no Miya|language=en}} It is not strictly speaking a {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=Moto-Ise Shrine|ja|元伊勢|1=Moto-Ise Shrines|simple|User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|en:draft|8=fr|9=Sanctuaires Moto-Ise|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto-Ise Shrines|qq=}} but it is a Moto-Geku shrine, this is to say Toyoukehime came to Earth in this shrine, and then moved to {{ill|Kono Shrine#Kago Shrine|lt=Kono Shrine|en|Kono Shrine#Kago Shrine|simple|User:Immanuelle/Kono Shrine#Kago Shrine|10=ja|dd=籠神社#奥宮（Keigai-Sessha）|lt2=籠神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kono Shrine#Kago Shrine|qq=}} and eventually got to Geku{{Cite web|title=Dragons, Demons and Deities: Folklore of the Kyoto by the Sea Area {{!}} Inspiration|url=https://www.kyototourism.org/en/column/17773/|access-date=2024-03-29|website=Another Kyoto Official Travel Guide|language=en-US}}. The {{Nihongo|{{ill|Hinumanai Shrine|ja|比沼麻奈為神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinumanai Shrine|qq=}}|比沼麻奈為神社}} is mentioned in [[Engishiki]] dating back to {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, as {{Nihongo||田庭|Taniwa}} literally meaning ''the Garden of Rice Paddies''. That ancient place name is thought to have changed over time to Taba (location of rice paddies), then to {{Nihongo|2=丹波|3=Tanba/Tanba}}. Nearby there is a well {{Nihongo|2=清水戸|3=Seisuido}} and a story of the now lost half-moon-shaped rice paddy {{Nihongo|2=月の輪田|3=Tsukinowa den}}. They are believed to be the site where Toyouke had soaked rice seeds to encourage germination and planted the first rice.{{Cite web |last=Sankei Digital Inc. |date=2014-10-21 |title=Inasaku hasshō no chi "Tsukinowa den" de inekari-shiki — Kyōtango |script-title=ja:稲作発祥の地「月の輪田」で稲刈り式 京丹後 |trans-title=The Moon paddy sees rice harvesting ceremony to be resumed — ōō |url=https://www.sankei.com/west/news/141021/wst1410210035-n1.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160831064938/https://www.sankei.com/west/news/141021/wst1410210035-n1.html |access-date=2019-12-08 |website=Sankei WEST |archive-date=2016-08-31 |language=ja}} However unfortunately due to the [[Shrine Consolidation Policy]] the original Tsukinowa den was likely destroyed. It venerates the deity Toyoke no Kami and is located at the foot of Mount Kujigatake. == Overview == To reach Hinumanai Shrine visitors follow a road that leads to its torii gate. Next to this entrance stands a group of stones guiding guests to a section within the shrine grounds. Interestingly there is no priest (guji san) at Hinumanai Shrine. The shrines layout features a [[Torii|torii gate]] at the foot of a staircase that meanders into an area and ultimately arrives at the haiden or worship hall. Positioned close, to the haiden is the [[Honden]] dedicated to Toyoke no Kami—a deity associated with agriculture and prosperity. A special monument honoring Toyoke no Kami stands on the side of the honden haiden complex. Visitors are advised against venturing beyond the keidai (shrine precincts) or climbing {{ill|Mount Isanago|ja|磯砂山|ceb|Isanago-yama|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Isanago|1=Mount Isanago|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q31707112}} to maintain the sanctity of this revered site. There have been discussions regarding the whereabouts of {{ill|Toyouke-hime|en|Toyouke-hime|qid=Q2983724|lt=Toyoke Omikami's|1=Toyouke-hime}} tomb. Access, to locations near the shrine is off limits. As visitors depart from the shrine they may observe papers fluttering on the rope of the torii gate adding to the tranquil ambiance of the shrine. While visitors are encouraged to show respect and reflect in silence certain paths and areas within its spiritual surroundings are restricted for exploration. ==overview== {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Hinumanai Shrine (比沼麻奈為神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Hinumanai-jinja, haiden.jpg|270px]]
Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=dd|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{coord|35|35|33.3|N|135|1|44.3|E}}}} | location = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|qid=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}, [[:en:Kyotango|Kyotango]], [[Mineyama]], Kutsugumiya-dani 510 | Latitude_deg = 35 | Latitude_min = 35 | Latitude_sec = 33.3 | Longitude_deg = 135 | Longitude_min = 1 | Longitude_sec = 44.3 | Map_code = JP | Map = Japan Kyoto | Label_position= right | deity = {{ill|Toyouke-hime|en|Toyouke-hime|qid=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime}} | Shrine_Rank = Former [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|village shrine]] | Establishment = | Main_hall = Single-bay shrine, [[Shinmei-zukuri]][https://www.kyotango.gr.jp/sightseeing/702/ Hinumanai Shrine] Kyotango Navi, thatched roof{{Cite book|title=Kyoto Shrine and Temple Architecture: Yosa and Tango Edition|publisher=Kyoto Cultural Property Protection Foundation|date=1985|page=150}} | Alias = | Festival_date = September 15{{Cite book|title=Kyoto Encyclopedia: Prefectural Edition|publisher=Tankosha|date=1994|page=441}} | Rituals = Otabi Festival, October 10 }} [[File:Hinumanai-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Hinumanai Shrine''' (比沼麻奈為神社) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|qid=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}, [[:en:Kyotango|Kyotango]], [[Mineyama]], Kutsugumiya-dani 510. The shrine's main enshrined deity is {{ill|Toyouke-hime|en|Toyouke-hime|qid=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime}}, revered as the god of agriculture and the guardian of the five grains. It is identified with the ancient [[Engishiki]]-designated shrine, Hinumanai Shrine of the Tanba district.{{Cite book|title=Traditions and Dialects of Kyotango|publisher=Kyotango City|date=2012|pages=70-71}} Under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]], it was classified as a village shrine. == Deities == ; {{ill|Toyouke-hime|en|Toyouke-hime|qid=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime}} (豊受大神) : The principal deity, Toyouke Ōmikami, is believed to have descended upon {{ill|Mount Isanago|ja|磯砂山|ceb|Isanago-yama|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Isanago|1=Mount Isanago|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q31707112}} (磯砂山) in what is now [[:en:Kyotango|Kyotango]]. Shrine records narrate, "In the distant age of the gods, the deity descended upon this land of Manaibara, cultivating fields and sowing rice, barley, beans, and other crops, while also introducing sericulture and weaving techniques."{{Cite book|title=Journey into Ancient Tango|author=Mamoru Ebara|publisher=Agenda Project|date=2004|page=132}} The deity is said to have been instrumental in the introduction of rice cultivation to the {{ill|Tango Province|en|Tango Province|qid=Q1054331|lt=Tango Province|1=Tango Province}} region. Other enshrined deities include: * [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi no Mikoto]] (瓊瓊杵尊) * [[Ame-no-Koyane|Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto]] (天児屋根命) * [[Futodama|Ame-no-Futodama no Mikoto]] (天太玉命) == History == The exact date of the shrine's establishment is unknown, but records of reconstruction survive. According to a copy of an old inscription, the original two-bay shrine was destroyed by fire in {{ill|1051|en|1051|qid=Q19363|lt=1051|1=1051}} (Eishō 6) and rebuilt in {{ill|1053|en|1053|qid=Q19368|lt=1053|1=1053}} (Tenki 1).{{Cite book|title=Mineyama Local History, Vol. II|publisher=Rinsen Bookstore|date=1964|page=420}} Subsequent reconstructions occurred in {{ill|1289|en|1289|qid=Q5551|lt=1289|1=1289}} (Shōō 2), {{ill|1491|en|1491|qid=Q6676|lt=1491|1=1491}} (Entoku 3), and {{ill|1705|en|1705|qid=Q6830|lt=1705|1=1705}} (Hōei 2). By the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, inscriptions indicate the shrine was known as "Hiji Manaibara Toyouke Taijingu." The current main hall, a single-bay [[Shinmei-zukuri]] structure, was rebuilt in {{ill|1922|en|1922|qid=Q2165|lt=1922|1=1922}} (Taishō dd). The shrine has been associated with [[Ise Grand Shrine]] as the original location of Toyouke Ōmikami before the deity was enshrined at the {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|zh|豐受大神宮|lt=Outer Shrine of Ise|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}. == Shrine Grounds == The shrine grounds span approximately 1,799 tsubo (approx. 5,946 m²). A monument inscribed by Kurita Kan commemorates Toyouke Ōmikami’s contributions. Sub-shrines include: * '''Inari Shrine''': Dedicated to [[Ukanomitama no Mikoto]] * '''Sada Shrine''': Dedicated to [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko no Mikoto]] * '''Akiha Shrine''': Dedicated to [[Kagu-tsuchi|Kagutsuchi]] == Annual Events == * '''New Year Festival''' – January 1 * '''Setsubun Festival''' – Early February * '''New Harvest Festival''' – December 1 * '''Main Festival''' – September 15; includes the offering of sacred dances ([[kagura]]) and the performance of traditional music called "Sasabayashi." == Access == * location: {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|qid=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}, [[:en:Kyotango|Kyotango]], [[Mineyama]], Kutsugumiya-dani 510 : Latitude 35.592636, Longitude 135.0290dd * By public transport: From {{ill|Mineyama Station|en|Mineyama Station|qid=Q6864579|lt=Mineyama Station|1=Mineyama Station}} (Kyoto Tango Railway), take a [[Tankai Bus]] to Nika; 20-minute walk. == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Further Reading == * "Mineyama Local History," Rinsen Bookstore, 1963-1964. * "Journey into Ancient Tango," Mamoru Ebara, Agenda Project, 2004. * "Kyoto Shrine and Temple Architecture," Kyoto Cultural Property Protection Foundation, 1985. == External Links == * [https://www.kyotango.gr.jp/sightseeing/702/ Hinumanai Shrine] – Kyotango Navi ==Gallery== File:Hinumanai-jinja, haiden.jpg|alt=拝殿|Haiden (Worship Hall) of Hinumanai-jinja File:Hinumanai-jinja, honden.jpg|alt=本殿|Honden (Main Shrine) of Hinumanai-jinja File:Hinumanai-jinja, keidaisha.jpg|alt=境内社|Keidaisha (Auxiliary Shrine) of Hinumanai-jinja File:Hinumanai-jinja, sandou.jpg|alt=参道|Sandou (Approach) to Hinumanai-jinja File:Hinumanai-jinja, shaden-1.jpg|alt=社殿-1|Shaden of Hinumanai-jinja File:Hinumanai-jinja, shaden-2.jpg|alt=社殿-2|Shaden of Hinumanai-jinja (alternate view) File:Hinumanai-jinja, torii.jpg|alt=鳥居|Torii (Shrine Gate) of Hinumanai-jinja File:Kyotofu Kyotangoshi Hinumanai jinja torii.jpg|alt=鳥居|Torii (Shrine Gate) of Hinumanai-jinja in Kyoto Tango City == References == {{reflist}} == Further reading == * Anzu, Motohiko. "{{Nihongo|2=外宮神道教学の展開|3=Geku shinto kyogaku no tenkai}}" {{in lang|ja}}. pp. 1–18, ''{{Nihongo|2=神道学|3=Shintogaku}}''. (79). {{ill|Taisha, Shimane|en|Taisha, Shimane|qid=Q7676403|lt=Taisha, Shimane|1=Taisha, Shimane}} : Shinto gakkai (ed.), November 1973, {{doi|10.dd501/2263701}}, {{ISSN|0583-0680}}. * "Inner shrine, Ise, early 1st cent. (rebuilt 1993)". {{ill|Winnipeg|en|Winnipeg|qid=Q2135|lt=Winnipeg|1=Winnipeg}} : {{ill|University of Manitoba|en|University of Manitoba|qid=Q1191833|lt=University of Manitoba|1=University of Manitoba}}, 2003. Series: UML Slide Survey set. {{OCLC|184769295}}. 1 black and white slide. * Kohori, Kunio; Hibi, Sadao. (1996) "{{Nihongo|Ise Shrine|伊勢神宮}}". Karābukkusu, 890, Tokyo : Hoikusha. {{ISBN|978-4-586-50890-7}}, {{OCLC|674695461}}. * Kohori, Kunio.(20dd) "{{Nihongo|2=伊勢神宮のこころ、式年遷宮の意味|3=Ise jingū no kokoro — shikinen sengū no imi}}". Kyoto : Tankōsha. {{ISBN|9784473036957|4473036952}}, {{OCLC|744282296}}. * {{ill|Stuart D. B. Picken|en|Stuart D. B. Picken|qid=Q7626949|lt=Picken, Stuart D. B.|1=Stuart D. B. Picken}} "Faith-Based Schools in Japan: Paradoxes and Pointers". Chapman, Judith D.; ''International Handbook of Learning, Teaching and Leading in Faith-Based Schools''; 515–531; Springer Netherlands : Dordrecht. {{doi|10.1007/978-94-017-8972-1_30}}, {{ISBN|978-94-017-8971-4|978-94-017-8972-1}}, {{OCLC|7327103281}}. {{Jmyth navbox long}}
